status_note: Renamed iSeries in 2000, then System i in 2006, then IBM i in 2008; platform still active
notes: Introduced May 1994; PowerPC AS-based models announced September 1995 for availability before end of 1995

status_note: PowerPC AS evolved into the POWER architecture series; POWER still active
notes: 64-bit RISC microprocessor at heart of new AS/400 Advanced Series models; also known as PowerPC AS

status_note: OS/400 renamed IBM i OS in 2008; still active
notes: Introduced to exploit 64-bit RISC architecture; required platform for SAP R/3 v3.0 on AS/400; implements Single Unix Specification (Spec 1170)

status_note: SAP R/3 replaced by SAP ERP (ECC) then SAP S/4HANA; R/3 support ended 2015
notes: Three-tier client-server ERP suite; version 3.0 announced for AS/400 September 1995; introduced 1992

status_note: SAP R/2 mainframe product eventually sunset
notes: SAP's mainframe-based predecessor product; still part of overall SAP strategy in 1995 but eclipsed by R/3

status_note: DB2/400 still active as Db2 for i on IBM i platform
notes: Integrated RDBMS on AS/400; ability to run SAP R/3 as 64-bit application supported by 64-bit DB2/400 cited as key advantage

status_note: ABAP/4 became ABAP; still used extensively in SAP development
notes: SAP's proprietary 4GL development language and workbench; used to develop all R/3 applications; provides database independence layer

status_note: Three-tier architecture remains standard enterprise pattern
notes: SAP R/3 deployed in three-tier client-server architecture with presentation/application/database separation

tech_name: R/3 Repository and SAP Information Model
status_note: Repository concept evolved into SAP BTP and S/4HANA metadata framework
notes: Central metadata and data dictionary; enables RDBMS portability; comprises Enterprise Data Model (EDM), Enterprise Functional Model (EFM), Organizational Model (OM)
IBM's midrange predecessor platform family (System/3, System/32, System/34, System/36, System/38); evolved into AS/400
SAP R/3 presentation layer supports Windows 3.1, Windows NT, and OS/2 GUIs simultaneously
